Created # Date First Name Last Name City State MTA Capital Projects Public Hearing FFY 2026 - Online Comments Received 1 1/16/2026 Jason Chen Staten Island NY Is there any possible way to remove the North Shore Branch, I think it would be a good idea for the North Shore to use the train instead of the buses to the ferry, there are a lot of abandoned infrastructure on the north shore so there is high potential that it could in use ~056762/6462/67 I never want to own a car and the subway is a big reason why I have lived in NYC for over two decades. Since becoming a parent four years ago, I have realized just how poor the accessibility of our system is compared to the rest of the world. Not everyone will need a wheelchair at some point in their life, but the needs for strollers or luggage are inevitable. We all benefit from elevators and ramps. Please demand more affordable accessibility so we can build more for our taxpayer money. Nearly $100 million to equip a single station is ludicrous. London and Paris are far older -- and more expensive cities -- that can build them more efficiently. Free buses championed by our new mayor would be great too, but please start with better service for those who pay fares. 2 1/17/2026 Adam Edwards Brooklyn NY https://nyc.streetsblog.org/2024/10/03/subway-elevators-are-not-just-a-basic-lift-but-a-basic-civil-right Please do not waste any more money on creating new turnstiles that still are ineffective at preventing fare jumpers. It is laughable easy for people to sneak past those, so IBC's just a waste of funds. Instead, please allocate all that money to repairing structural issues throughout the system. 3 1/19/2026 Shana Caro Brooklyn NY Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, Mr. Farrell, and Members of the MTA Board, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to an existing order for clean-diesel buses awarded in late-2025, and a proposal I have regarding future plans for the fleet in regards to deliveries and depot assignments. In this message/letter, I am referring directly to the contracts of 58 New Flyer XD40, 161 New Flyer XD640, and 100 Nova LFS buses that were awarded by the MTA in November and December 2025 respectively. These clean-diesel buses should be assigned to bus depots such as Castleton, Casey Stengel, Flatbush, and Ulmer Park depots, and the reason behind these assignments is due to the fact that these bus depots currently lack the appropriate EV infrastructure to power electric buses. Here are the bus numbers that each of the buses in the contracts should be assigned: 58 New Flyer XD40: 3761-5818 161 New Flyer XD640: 3600-3760 100 Nova LFS: 7990-8089 For the Nova LFS order slated for deliveries in 2027, the order would inherit the fleet numbers from the 2011 Nova LFS buses which currently have fleet numbers 8000-8089. Because of this, the 2011 Nova LFS buses must be retired immediately following deliveries of the 2027 Nova LFS buses, however the first set of buses that should arrive would be the 7990-7999 batch, fleet numbers that are currently vacant. In terms of the depot assignments, these are the proposed depot assignments, which are as follows: New Flyer XD40 (58 bus order): Please note that all 58 XD40s would be going to Castleton to replace the 2011 Orion VII EPA10s. 3761-3818 - Castleton New Flyer XD640 (161 bus order): These buses would be allocated to both Flatbush and Ulmer Park depots to replace the 2011 Orion EPA10s and remaining Orion VII NGs at Flatbush and 2011-2012 New Flyer XD40s and 2014-2015 New Flyer XD40s at Ulmer Park, as well as enable for fleet expansion at both Flatbush and Ulmer Park depots. 3600-3676 - Flatbush 3679-3760 - Ulmer Park Nova LFS (100 bus order): These buses would be allocated to both Castleton and Casey Stengel depots to replace the 2011 Nova LFS at Casey Stengel and the 2011 Orion VII EPA10s at Castleton, as well as enable for fleet expansion at both Castleton and Casey Stengel. 7990-8039 - Castleton 8040-8089 - Casey Stengel These proposed bus assignments would ensure that the MTA can distribute these clean-diesel buses across bus depots in Queens, Brooklyn, and Staten Island to replace their respective aging bus fleets as an interim solution while getting these buses as fast as the appropriate EV infrastructure to build a permanent electric future in the long term. 4 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, and Mr. Farrell, I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to propose a comprehensive strategy for the acquisition and integration of private commuter bus services, specifically Academy Bus Lines' commuter division and Coach USA's routes (under Renco Group), into a unified MTA Express Bus network under the New York City Transit Authority (NYCTA). This initiative must also include the acquisition of Private Transportation Corporation and the integration of the B110 bus route and the full incorporation of the MTA Bus Company into NYCTA, thereby streamlining operations, enhancing rider experience, and expanding MTA's regional reach. In this proposal, the MTA should acquire the commuter division of Academy Bus Lines, Coach USA (under Renco Group), and Private Transportation Corporation (operates the B110 bus) and integrate all these including the MTA Bus Company (MTABC) into a unified New York City Transit Authority (NYCTA). Since some of the existing routes being acquired from Academy and Renco Group have multiple terminuses, the routes should be split up to create multiple routes which should include two terminuses (one in NYC, one in Upstate) for enhanced efficiency which the MTA would need feedback during the integration. The initial phase must focus on transitioning Academy Bus Lines' commuter operations. This involves acquiring their assets and integrating their routes, such as those from Turnpike (Exit 9A), Lakewood/Old Bridge, Toms River, Point Pleasant/Manasquan, and Willingboro/Westampton, into the MTA fare structure, including OMNY. These routes would operate 24/7, be rebranded under the MTA, and be assigned new NJM codes like NJM1, NJM2, NJM5, NJM11, and NJM15 to reflect their New Jersey to Manhattan express bus service. The second phase must involve integrating Coach USA's 53 routes that serve the Port Authority Bus Terminal (PABT). This includes 33 existing NJ-NY routes and 20 Upstate NY-NYC routes. All these services would adopt OMNY, receive MTA branding, and operate 24/7. They would be assigned new NJM and NYM codes, for example, NJM41 for the Princeton, NJ to PABT route (formerly Suburban Transit Line 100), and NYM3 for the West Nyack, NY to PABT route. Additionally, a new express route, NYM1, would be established to connect PABT directly to Woodbury Common Outlets, utilizing acquired Coach USA assets. The final critical components of this proposal involve integrating the B110 bus route and the MTA Bus Company. The B110, currently the last NYC DOT-supervised route not under MTA control, must be acquired and redesigned. A new B110 route along Bedford Avenue connecting Borough Park and Williamsburg should be created, or its stops integrated into existing routes, to ensure OMNY acceptance, 24/7 operation, and adherence to MTA service standards and anti-discrimination policies. Concurrently, the MTA Bus Company should be fully absorbed into NYCTA, consolidating bus operations and ensuring a singular, cohesive management structure for all city and express bus services. This comprehensive integration strategy promises significant benefits: a seamless rider experience through unified branding and OMNY; expanded regional coverage with 24/7 service; enhanced operational efficiencies via streamlined management, optimized routes, and a larger, flexible fleet; and modernized, consistent fleet standards across the entire integrated network. This will create a truly world-class, responsive public transportation system for all New Yorkers and regional commuters. Thank you for considering this proposal. Sincerely, Tran Jacinto Request: Please forward this to MTA Chair Janno Lieber, MTA President Demetrios Crichlow, MTA Acting Executive Vice President of Department of Buses/MTA Bus Company, and the entire MTA Board for review. I kindly urge you to consider this proposal to further enhance bus service and reliability to meet diverse demand. Thank you. 5 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, Mr. Farrell, and Members of the MTA Board, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to the MTA Express Bus fleet, and a proposal I have regarding future plans for the express bus fleet in regards to deliveries and depot assignments. In this message/letter, I am referring directly to the exercised options from the 1630-1879 order, which will have fleet numbers 1880-2010 and a future contract for 92 MCI D45 CRT buses and 5 MCI D45 CRT CHARGE buses. The exercised order for additional express buses from the 1630-1879 order which will be slated for fleet numbers 1880-2010, should be assigned to Queens Village, Yupon, and Ulmer Park to replace the 2011 Prevost X3-45 buses and the 2012 MCI D4500CT buses, as the 1880-2010 order will operate exclusively within NYCTA. The garage assignments should be as follows: Proposed Garage Assignments for 1880-2010 Order: 1880-1912 - Queens Village 1913-1950 - Yupon 1951-2010 - Ulmer Park In regards to the contract for 92 MCI D45 CRT buses which was awarded by the MTA in April 2024, the MTA should reassign the contract to Prevost for 92 additional Prevost X3-45 motorcoaches. However, the order for 5 MCI D45 CRT CHARGE electric motorcoaches for testing and evaluation should remain unchanged, since it would only be the order in the contract for 92 MCI clean-diesel motorcoaches that should be reassigned to Prevost for the X3-45. The reason why Contract #840721 for 92 MCI clean-diesel buses should be reassigned to Prevost is to enable the MTA to achieve fleet standardization, reliability, operational efficiency, and streamlined maintenance. The MTA should have the contract for the 92 MCI D45 CRT buses reassigned to Prevost at a March 2026 MTA Board Meeting, for deliveries for both the 92 buses reassigned to Prevost, alongside the five pilot battery-electric express buses taking place in the Spring of 2026, all for the 2026 model year. These additional buses should replace some 2014-2016 Prevost X3-45s that have already been retired, alongside allowing for the remaining 2014-2016 Prevost X3-45s expected to remain in service to be reassigned to other bus depots. The 92 additional buses once reassigned to Prevost for the X3-45 should have fleet numbers 2011-2102, while the D45 CRTs pilots should have fleet numbers 2103-2107. All D45 CRTs pilot buses should be based at Ulmer Park, alongside the 1951-2010 and 2011-2102 batch. The garage assignments for these buses should be as follows: Proposed Garage Assignments for 2011-2102 Order: 2011-2030 - Ulmer Park 2031-2058 - Queens Village 2059-2102 - Yupon Unlike express buses that the MTA has operated historically, the MTA should equip the five D45 CRT pilot buses to feature a rear door, similar to what was seen on the 2018 MCI D45 CRT LE demo bus that the MTA operated back in 2018 when it used the demo bus to evaluate a new design for enhanced wheelchair accessibility on express bus routes. This should be used not only to test electric buses on express bus routes as part of a mandate for all MTA buses to be zero-emission by 2040, but to also test all-door usage on express bus routes, something not possible with the single-door designs found on existing express buses. To conclude, these garage assignments for the Prevost X3-45 buses in the 1880-2010 and 2011-2102 bus orders are 6 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY
```

## Page 287

[p287_c0001]

```text
I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to an existing order for clean-diesel articulated buses, and a proposal I have regarding future plans for the fleet in regards to deliveries and depot assignments. In this message/letter, I am referring directly to the exercised options from the 6287-6510 order, which will have fleet numbers 6511-6596. The exercised order for additional clean diesel articulated buses from the 6287-6510 order which will be slated for fleet numbers 6511-6596, should be assigned to Queens Village, Yonkers, and Ulmer Park to replace the 2013 New Flyer XD60 buses and the remaining 2013 Nova LFSAs buses, as the 6511-6596 order will operate exclusively within NYCTA. As the current 6287-6510 order will consist of buses in the 2025, 2026, and 2027 model years, the buses in the 6511-6596 order should consist of buses from the 2027 and 2028 model years, and deliveries should start once deliveries in the base 6287-6510 order are complete. The garage assignments should be as follows: 6511-6585 - Tuskegee Airmen 6586-6590 - Kingsbridge 6591-6760 - Flatbush 6761-6815 - Casey Stengel 6816-6855 - Grand Avenue 6856-6894 - West Farms 6895-6956 - Ulmer Park Based on these assignments, most of these depots currently operate either 2011/2013 Nova LFSAs and/or 2013 New Flyer XD60 buses. However, since Flatbush doesn't operate any of these buses and should be entitled to receive some buses in the 6511-6596 option order, its currently entire existing fleet of articulated buses currently consisting of 2017/2019 New Flyer XD60s and 2018 Nova LFSAs should be transferred to other bus depots that are currently facing a shortage of articulated buses, and the reason why Flatbush should receive some buses from the 6511-6596 option order from the 6287-6510 order is to enable that depot to streamline its operations with one bus type from one bus order and model year, as well as to operate slightly more articulated buses in order to increase frequencies on the existing R44 S8S and R46 S8S routes, both of which are currently facing high ridership demand and is crucial for the existing articulated buses over at Flatbush to be transferred to other depots and for Flatbush to receive brand-new articulated buses since its current bus fleet is not enough to meet high ridership demand on those routes, especially when it comes to accessibility needs, as high ridership routes result in the need for more improved accessibility. To conclude, these garage assignments for the XD60s in the exercised order are crucial because this determines which buses should be assigned to which depot, based on the amount of 2013 New Flyer XD60s and 2013 Nova LFSAs that each depot currently operates that are slated for retirement thanks to the arrival of these brand-new electric buses. I urge the MTA Board to consider these suggestions to ensure high-quality public transit with these proposed garage assignments for the New Flyer XD60s in the 6511-6596 order, options from the existing 6287-6510 order. Thank you. Sincerely, 7 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Tran Jacinto Dear Members of the MTA Board, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to a proposal regarding which subway line the MTA should deploy the R211 next and a proposal regarding rolling stock assignment changes, as a result of these R211 deployments. The reason behind these changes is to allow the MTA to improve service reliability and take advantage of the operational synergies between two subway lines. In this proposal, the MTA should begin deploying the R211A on the D line in 2026. This would allow the MTA to deploy a CBTC equipped fleet in the rolling stock onto a subway line that is slated to receive CBTC in the future. This proposal is also highly beneficial, especially since the B line already operates R211A subway cars. Along with this, the MTA should aim to have both B and D lines combined to operate exclusively R211A subway cars by 2027. Because of this proposal, the transition to achieving a 100% R211 operation on both the B and D lines combined should be implemented in phases. This should see the introduction of R211 on the D line in April 2026. By July 1, 2027, the MTA should expect to have both the B and D lines combined to operate a fleet of 100% R211 subway cars. Because of the introduction of brand new R211A subway cars on the D line due to CBTC on the Sixth Avenue Line, the MTA should begin removing the R68 and R68A subway cars from the B and D lines starting in 2026, which the discontinuation of R68 and R68A service from these subway lines should be implemented gradually. This should see the discontinuation of R68A service from the B line in June 2026, followed by the discontinuation of R68A service from the D line a year later in June 2027. As a result of the removal of the gradual R68/A trains from the B and D lines, the MTA should relocate these trains onto the N and Q lines, in order to expedite R46 retirements. In addition to the removal of R68/A service on both the B and D lines, the MTA should also relocate the R211A subway cars running on the B, D, and G line from Coney Island Yard to Concourse Yard, in order to achieve fleet consolidation and offer more streamlined maintenance. This would provide room for all R68s that currently operate on the D line and are currently based at Concourse Yard in Coney Island Yard for each Concourse-based R68 subway car transferred to Coney Island Yard for service on the N and Q lines. To conclude, the proposed phased deployment and fleet reassignment strategy for the R211A, R68A, and R46 trains provides a clear and practical roadmap for improving the MTA's operational efficiency. By consolidating the R211A fleet on the B and D lines, the Authority can not only prepare for future CBTC signaling but also streamline maintenance and service reliability. The subsequent relocation of R68A cars will effectively expedite the retirement of the older R46 fleet. This comprehensive plan is a strategic and beneficial step toward modernizing the subway system and ultimately enhancing the overall experience for riders. I strongly urge you to consider these suggestions to ensure high-quality public transit with these proposals. Thank you. Sincerely, 8 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Tran Jacinto Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, and Members of the MTA Board, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to a significant proposal for the deinterlining of the 59th Street Junction, a critical juncture in the New York City subway system, which I believe offers a practical and highly effective solution to long-standing operational challenges. My deinterlining proposal for the 59th Street Junction is designed to simplify train movements and enhance efficiency by re-routing key lines. Specifically, the A train would be re-routed to 168th Street, effectively replacing the C train on that segment. The B train would be re-routed to Inwood-207th Street, replacing the A train's current terminal. Concurrently, the C train would be re-routed to Bedford Park Boulevard, taking over the segment currently served by the B train during rush hours. The D train would maintain its current route to Norwood-205th Street. This precise realignment aims to deinterline the 59th Street Junction without necessitating extensive system-wide modifications. A key aspect of this proposal is the extension of these service pattern changes to operate daily at all times (7 days a week, 24/7), ensuring consistent service for all commuters. This consistent routing and operation for the B, C, and D lines would lead to increased service frequency and capacity, alongside improved reliability. With fewer trains contending for the same track space at the 59th Street Junction, the MTA can achieve more consistent headways and potentially increase the number of trains per hour on the affected lines. This not only translates to reduced waiting times for commuters but also makes the entire system more resilient to unforeseen disruptions, as incidents on one line will be less likely to propagate across the network. Furthermore, a significant advantage of this proposal is its high feasibility and potential for cost-effectiveness. Deinterlining at the 59th Street Junction, unlike some other large-scale infrastructure projects, can largely be achieved through operational and scheduling adjustments. The focus shifts from costly civil engineering to intelligent traffic management, leveraging existing infrastructure to unlock significant performance improvements for millions of daily commuters. The MTA should aim to have this deinterlining plan finalized by 2027 upon Board Approval and have the deinterlining plan taken into effect in January 2028, in which commuters and people in the community would have to be notified regarding such service changes before they can take effect. In conclusion, I urge you to consider the immense potential of this 59th Street Junction deinterlining proposal to fundamentally improve the reliability and efficiency of our subway system. I believe this strategic adjustment offers a pragmatic path forward to "Help People Get Smart Faster" by ensuring they reach their destinations with greater punctuality and less frustration. I am available to discuss this proposal further and provide any additional information that may be helpful. Thank you for your consideration. I hope to see these ideas come to reality. Sincerely, Tran Jacinto Request: Please forward this to MTA Chair Mr. Janno Lieber, MTA President Mr. Demetrios Crichlow, and the entire MTA Board for review and consideration, as this message is intended for them. Also, please consider these specific changes as detailed above to help improve service on subway lines that use the 59th Street Junction. Thank you. 9 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Tran Jacinto Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, and Members of the MTA Board, I hope this message finds you well. I am writing in regards to a significant proposal for the deinterlining of the DeKalb Junction, a critical juncture in the New York City subway system, which I believe offers a practical and highly effective solution to long-standing operational challenges. My deinterlining proposal for DeKalb Junction is designed to simplify train movements and enhance efficiency by re-routing key lines. Specifically, the B train would be re-routed to Bay Ridge-96th Street, effectively replacing the R train on that segment. The D, N, and Q trains would maintain their current routes to Coney Island-Stillwell Avenue via West End (D), Sea Beach (N), and Brighton (Q), respectively. Concurrently, the R train would be re-routed to Brighton Beach, taking over the segment currently served by the B train. This precise realignment aims to deinterline the DeKalb Junction without necessitating extensive system-wide modifications. The benefits of implementing this deinterlining proposal are substantial, ranging from increased service frequency and capacity to improved reliability. With fewer trains contending for the same track space at DeKalb, the MTA can achieve more consistent headways and potentially increase the number of trains per hour on the affected lines. This not only translates to reduced waiting times for commuters but also makes the entire system more resilient to unforeseen disruptions, as incidents on one line will be less likely to propagate across the network. A key aspect of this proposal is the extension of these service pattern changes to operate daily at all times (7 days a week, 24/7), ensuring consistent service for all commuters. This consistent routing and operation for the B, D, N, Q, and R lines would lead to increased service frequency and capacity, alongside improved reliability. Furthermore, a key advantage of this proposal is its high feasibility and potential for cost-effectiveness. The deinterlining at DeKalb Junction, unlike some other large-scale infrastructure projects, can largely be achieved through operational and scheduling adjustments. The focus shifts from costly civil engineering to intelligent traffic management, leveraging existing infrastructure to unlock significant performance improvements for millions of daily commuters. The MTA should aim to have this deinterlining plan finalized by 2026 upon Board Approval and have the deinterlining plan taken into effect in the Summer of 2027, in which commuters and people in the community would have to be notified regarding such service changes before they can take effect. In conclusion, I urge you to consider the immense potential of this DeKalb Junction deinterlining proposal to fundamentally improve the reliability and efficiency of our subway system. I believe this strategic adjustment offers a pragmatic path forward to "Help People Get Smart Faster" by ensuring they reach their destinations with greater punctuality and less frustration. I am available to discuss this proposal further and provide any additional information that may be helpful. Thank you for your consideration. I hope to see these ideas come to reality. Sincerely, Tran Jacinto Request: Please forward this to MTA Chair Mr. Janno Lieber, MTA President Mr. Demetrios Crichlow, and the entire MTA Board for review and consideration, as this message is intended for them. Also, please consider these specific changes as detailed above to help improve service on subway lines that use the DeKalb Junction. Thank you. 10 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Tran Jacinto
```

Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, and Members of the MTA Board. I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to submit a formal follow-up to my previous correspondence (Case #07033140) regarding the final phase of the MetroCard-to-OMNY transition. As we have entered 2026, it is imperative that the MTA establishes a definitive "System Sunset" date to finalize the OMNY rollout and streamline transit operations. In my previous message, you didn't understand what I meant which is why I am following up this case. In this proposal, I propose that the MTA formally cease acceptance of all MetroCards and onboard bus coin payment effective July 1, 2026, at 12:00 AM. This date provides a clean break for the new fiscal quarter and allows sufficient time for riders to exhaust balances following the end of MetroCard sales in late 2025. To ensure a seamless transition, I propose a final, comprehensive OMNY system stability upgrade during the weekend of June 26th-29, 2026, to optimize the backend for 100% rider capacity and eliminate legacy technical bugs, which the final comprehensive OMNY system stability and software upgrade should take place between June 26 at 11:00PM and June 29 at 5:00AM. Starting July 1, 2026, all magnetic swipers on turnstiles and "dippers" on buses should be immediately disabled as an interim measure. By July 31, 2026, all legacy hardware, including onboard bus coin-handling units should be physically removed and replaced with permanent, flush metal plating to signify the end of the mechanical payment era. Furthermore, for the 2026/2027 school year, I propose a new feature allowing students to integrate their school-issued physical OMNY cards into their digital wallets via a simple photo-verification process in the OMNY app. In addition to the current plan for riders to shift their remaining MetroCard balance at MTA customer service centers to their new OMNY cards, I propose incentivizing this shift and support low-income residents by offering a $20 transition credit be added to the new OMNY card in addition to the transferred MetroCard balance once the MTA stops accepting MetroCards and coins as a form of fare payment as of July 1, 2026. While the legal window for transfers may remain open longer and is scheduled to operate in a two-year period, the MTA should launch a campaign to get riders who still have a MetroCard with remaining balance left to transfer their balance into an OMNY card by December 31, 2026. This proposed campaign would ensure the vast majority of riders are fully integrated into the OMNY ecosystem by the start of 2027 and should aim for all riders to have already transferred their remaining MetroCard balances onto an OMNY card before the start of the 2027 new year. The proposed campaign should be launched following the proposed cutoff date. This proposal represents a significant milestone for the MTA. By setting a hard deadline and providing a clear path for students and low-income riders, the MTA can finally complete its transition to a world-class, 100% contactless system. I strongly urge the entire MTA Board to consider these suggestions to ensure the highest quality of public transit for all New Yorkers and have this proposal including the proposed cutoff date of July 1, 2026 at 12AM announced in the coming weeks, possibly at a future MTA Board Meeting. Thank you for your time and continued dedication to our city's infrastructure. Sincerely, Tran Jacinto 11 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Request: Please forward this to MTA Chair Janno Lieber, MTA President Demetrios Crichlow, and the entire MTA Board for review, as this urgent letter is intended for them. Thank you. Dear Chair Lieber, Mr. Crichlow, Mr. Farrell, and Members of the MTA Board. I hope you are well. I am writing to outline a proposed addition to the MTA's zero&0 emission bus plan, to be considered if the ongoing hydrogen fuel&cell bus pilot is successful. This proposal calls for integrating hydrogen fuel&cell buses into the MTA's 2040 zero&0 emissions fleet strategy alongside battery&electric buses. Under this plan, hydrogen buses would be assigned to five depots&one in each borough. They would primarily replace CNG buses; however, because Manhattan and Staten Island do not operate CNG fleets, one diesel/hybrid depot in each borough would require upgrades to support hydrogen operations. The proposal suggests announcing this initiative on Earth Day with Governor Kathy Hochul, followed by the launch of a procurement for new hydrogen buses. On April 22, 2026, the MTA and Governor Hochul would present an implementation and investment plan to introduce hydrogen fuel&cell buses at Jackie Gleason (Brooklyn), West Farms (Bronx), Manhattanville (Manhattan), College Point (Queens), and Yonkers (Staten Island). At the same time, the MTA would open a procurement for 800 new hydrogen fuel&cell buses to replace aging CNG buses and the diesel/hybrid fleets at the Manhattan and Staten Island depots selected for conversion. The standard procurement process which consists of RFP release, proposal preparation, technical evaluation, negotiations, and Board review&0 would follow, with the goal of awarding the contract in the second half of 2027. If awarded on schedule, deliveries would begin in 2030 and conclude in 2032, covering model years 2030&0&2032. To differentiate long&term hydrogen fleets from battery&electric buses, the proposal recommends using three&0&0 eight fleet numbers, consistent with current CNG fleets, rather than the four&0&0 digit numbers used for the two pilot hydrogen buses and most of the MTA fleet. The five depots listed above were selected because they operate routes historically served by CNG buses and frequently exceed 300 miles per day, conditions suited to hydrogen buses' longer range and faster refueling, implementing hydrogen operations at five depots would require a major amendment to the MTA's $68.4&0 billion 2026&0&2029 Capital Program, which is currently structured around battery&electric buses and does not include large&0 scale hydrogen infrastructure. Advancing such an amendment would require the MTA to prepare a detailed internal proposal outlining scope, cost, and operational benefits, and demonstrating alignment with the 2040 zero&0 emissions mandate. The MTA Board would then need to approve the proposal before submitting it to the New York State Capital Program Review Board (CPRB), whose unanimous approval is required for major program modifications and for issuing bonds or accessing federal funds. A significant challenge would be securing billions in new funding, as the current capital plan is fully allocated. This would require identifying internal savings, pursuing federal or state clean&0 transportation grants, or obtaining legislative authorization for additional bonding or dedicated revenue. Because the MTA is only beginning to test hydrogen technology through the pilot at Gun Hill Depot, expansion to five hydrogen&0 capable depots would depend on the pilot&0 results, the development of a viable funding strategy, and integration with the existing plan to deploy more than 1,000 zero&0 emission buses and electrify 11 depots by 2029. Thank you for your time and consideration. Sincerely, Tran Jacinto 12 1/19/2026 Tran Jacinto Brooklyn NY Request: Please forward this to MTA Chair Janno Lieber, MTA President Demetrios Crichlow, Acting MTA Executive Vice President for Department of Buses Frank Farrell, and the entire MTA Board for review. Thank you. samfatz@gmail.com 21 1/27/2026 Cristina Julian Brooklyn NY samfatz@gmail.com 22 1/27/2026 Samuel Patz Brooklyn NY Please allocate funds to open the EXISTING elevators entrance/exits on the Gates Subway and Kozicowski stops. Gates and Halaxey stations have seen significant increases in passenger numbers and single exits are increasingly congested. 23 1/27/2026 Samuel Patz Brooklyn NY When are we addressing the noise pollution - dirt and grime. I come home from the subway and blow my nose and i&0s&0s back from the stuff in the air. I consider these extremely important Q&0L points. 24 1/29/2026 Ali Riv New York NY The MTA should not receive funding in the millions when service quality has severely disintegrated and continues to worsen. Riders across the system, especially in historically underserved neighborhoods, face constant train delays, cancellations, overcrowding, &0 unpredictable service. For many, this is not a minor inconvenience but a daily hardship that directly affects their ability to get to work, attend school, access healthcare, and meet basic life obligations. Despite repeated fare increases and massive public subsidies, service reliability continues to decline. This raises serious concerns about whether funds are being allocated responsibly, efficiently, and transparently. Riders are continually asked to pay more while receiving less, with little evidence that current funding levels are producing meaningful improvements in reliability, safety, or infrastructure maintenance. Underserved communities bear a disproportionate burden of this failure. These neighborhoods experience more frequent disruptions, longer commute times, and fewer dependable transit options, reinforcing long-standing social and economic inequities. Public transportation for a tool for mobility and opportunity, yet the current system consistently fails those who depend on it the most. Until the MTA can demonstrate true accountability, operational competence, and measurable service improvements, it should not receive additional capital funding. Taxpayer dollars should not continue flowing to an organization that fails to deliver reliable results. Public funding is a privilege that requires transparency, fiscal responsibility, and tangible outcomes. NOT vague promises, shifting timelines, and repeated failures. Without fundamental reform, increased funding will only perpetuate inefficiency, waste, and mismanagement at the expense of riders. The MTA must prioritize service quality, equity, and financial accountability before requesting any further public investment. 25 1/29/2026 Paige Serrano Brooklyn NY

DEBT MTA Board and Staff, I am submitting this comment regarding the MTA&C's proposed federal grant projects for Federal Fiscal Year 2026. As a daily rider and paying customer, I am concerned that federal funds should prioritize safety, reliability, and accessibility of transit service. Recent incidents, such as severe overcrowding and delays on bus number 6142 at Chauncey Street and Malcolm X Boulevard, created unsafe conditions for passengers and required police intervention. Extended delays and lack of contingency planning disproportionately affect riders, particularly in extreme weather, and undermine public confidence in the MTA. With federal funding proposed through Sections 5307, 5337, 5339, and related programs, I urge the MTA to: 1. Ensure timely and reliable service by investing in fleet expansion, maintenance, and operational improvements. 2. Prioritize rider safety and crowd management, including training and equipment to prevent unsafe boarding and overcrowding situations. 3. Address accessibility gaps, ensuring all riders&C including those with disabilities&C can use transit safely and reliably. 4. Allocate funds transparently, with measurable outcomes tied to improving day-to-day service for paying customers. Federal funds provide an opportunity to address recurring safety and service issues, especially as fares increase. I strongly encourage the MTA to direct these funds toward projects that directly improve the rider experience and prevent unsafe conditions. Thank you for considering this comment. Sincerely, Alexis M. Harris-Bates 28 1/29/2026 Alexis Harris-Bates Good evening, My name is Jesse Figueras. I am a US Army Veteran, an Accessibility and Transportation Advocate, and the District 1 Commander for the Veterans of Foreign Wars, Department of New York. As a resident of Bay Ridge, Brooklyn, I am here to strongly support the MTA&C's proposed projects for Federal Fiscal Year 2026. For the veterans and seniors I represent, the $3.28 billion requested for passenger stations and $12.5 billion for New York City Transit are not just numbers&C they are essential investments in accessibility and urban equity. We must prioritize "State of Good Repair" and signal upgrades to ensure our system remains a reliable alternative to the personal automobile. Federal funding through Sections 5307 and 5337 is the lifeblood of our city&C's mobility. I urge the FTA to recognize that the recovery of the New York metropolitan region depends on a fully funded, modernized transit network. 29 1/29/2026 Jesse Figueras Brooklyn NY Thank you for your time and for allowing me the opportunity to comment. I urge the FTA to recognize that the recovery of the New York metropolitan region depends on a fully funded, modernized transit network. 30 1/30/2026 Jesse Figueras Brooklyn NY Thank you for your time and for allowing me the opportunity to comment. Jesse Figueras PO BOX 90513 Brooklyn, New York 11209 wyepost46@outlook.com 718.912.8321 3 February 2024 MTA Capital Program Funding / Grants Management RE: 2026 Capital Projects Public Hearing 2 Broadway, D16.94 New York, NY 10004 Subject: Formal Statement in Support of MTA Federal Grant Proposals for FFY 2026 To the Metropolitan Transportation Authority and the Federal Transit Administration: Please accept this formal statement as part of the permanent record for the Capital Projects Public Hearing for Federal Fiscal Year 2026. I am writing to express my strong support for the MTA&C's proposed program of projects and to urge the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) to approve and fully fund the requested grants under Sections 5307, 5309, 5310, 5337, 5339, and 5340. The proposed investments are essential for maintaining the State of Good Repair and ensuring the continued reliability of our regional transit network. I would like to highlight several critical areas of the proposal: 3&C New York City Transit Modernization: The $12.58 billion requested for New York City Transit&C specifically the $6.85 billion for new subway cars and $3.28 billion for passenger stations&C is vital for addressing aging infrastructure and improving the commuter experience for millions. 3&C Regional Connectivity: The funding requests for the Long Island Rail Road ($624 million) and Metro-North Railroad ($770.32 million) are necessary to maintain safety and efficiency across the commuter rail lines that link our regional economy. 3&C Infrastructure Resiliency: The focus on track and structure repairs, particularly for Metro-North ($268.3 million), is a prudent step toward safeguarding our system against the increasing challenges posed by extreme weather and environmental factors. These projects are not only critical for daily mobility but also comply with the State Implementation Plan (SIP) and the Clean Air Act, supporting our region&C's environmental and sustainability goals. Thank you for your dedication to the preservation and improvement of the New York metropolitan area's transit system and for your consideration of these comments. Sincerely, Jesse Figueras 31 1/30/2026 Jesse Figueras Brooklyn NY Please continue the second avenue subway west across 125th BUT ALSO keep a provision for a spur to the East Bronx. 32 1/30/2026 Felipe Castillo New York NY I am writing in full support of Mayor Maman&C's free buses initiative and I would like to inquire as to whether the purchase of new buses is conducive to bringing this to fruition. Working people need a reliable form of fast, free, reliable transit in order to power this city. I would also like to voice support for expansion of lines and new track, although I do not see it in the proposed budget and I would like to voice my concern. This would be transformative for New Yorkers. Lastly, I would like to comment on the ineffectiveness of new turnstiles to prevent fare evasion as unpopular, wasteful use of public funds, and an embarrassing failure when more crucial repairs could be made to ensure safety of New Yorkers when riding the trains (prevent train derailing accidents like that happened on 1 train last year, mental health crisis counseling to help those that left with no help, jump on tracks, or perhaps simply making buses free to reduce cost of living crisis strain for all). The economic value generated by making these improvements would many times over make up for the shortfall of fare evaders. Thank you for your consideration. 33 1/31/2026 Grace Murray Brooklyn NY The newer R211A and R211L subway cars have about 90 seats per car. The still-used R179 has about 40-44 seats per car, while retiring cars like the R46 had around 70-76 seats per car. I would consider this a step in the wrong direction, and believe that seating in newer models should be able to accommodate an amount closer the what the R46 carried while still providing the newly-added accessible seating/areas. With feedback from riders expressing disappointment over the seating changes, would the MTA consider any redesign or resolutions that could increase seating? 34 2/2/2026 Damino Townes Brooklyn NY

Good evening. My name is Alexis, and I&#x27;m a daily bus rider. According to the MTA&#x27;s previous behavior, it is normal and acceptable to punish and intimidate riders for circumstances they didn&#x27;t create. Just today, after waiting 30 minutes in cold, the b4&#x27;select bus number 6110, finally arrived and was immediately overcrowded due to the usual delays on this route. MTA transit enforcement officers boarded the bus as well to make sure everyone paid. I witnessed a transit officer ask a rider to move over however, he physically was not able to because people were packed into the bus like sardines. The MTA officers then wrongfully accused him of being disorderly and threatened to stop the bus. Instead of addressing the overcrowding and the delayed service of the b4&#x27;, the entire bus was put out of service forcing riders, including the elderly and schoolchildren, back into the cold after we all just paid the 3.00 dollar fare. Last week on the same bus route, there were severe delays that left riders waiting desperately in the freezing temperatures hoping that any bus would show up. When the b4&#x27;select bus finally arrived it became extremely overcrowded, so NYPD officers were called to force people off the bus to wait in the frigid cold again. Now, a week after a major snowstorm, many MTA bus stops and bus shelters are still not cleared of snow and ice which continues to negatively impact the accessibility of public transit. I have video documenting MTA transit officer badge #853 irate, unprofessional, harassing people including schoolchildren. Air circulation aboard a car is often poor; I have personally measured CO2 levels above 1000 ppm a number of time, and occasionally over 2000 ppm. Given that many airborne diseases like measles are on the rise, the best time to upgrade filtration and ventilation was mid-2020. The second best time is as soon as possible. February 3, 2026 Re: Rail Infrastructure Investment Needs in Suffolk County Dear Chair Leader and Members of the MTA Board, Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the MTA&#x27;s proposed 2026 federally funded projects. I have reviewed the proposed investments. While many of the projects are necessary to maintain safe and reliable operations, they do not go far enough to meet the needs of Suffolk County. Suffolk County requires a revitalized 21st-century rail system and modernization of rail service to meet the current and future needs of our residents, businesses, and visitors. With more than 1.5 million residents, Suffolk is the fourth most populous county in New York State and the most populous county outside the boroughs of New York City. It is time to get serious about modernizing the LIRR in Suffolk County. Despite our many assets&#x27;high-quality school districts, premier research institutions, nationally ranked universities, and abundant parks and natural resources&#x27;much of Suffolk County remains dependent on diesel rail service and outdated technology which is the only local rail service option for Suffolk County residents east of Babylon, east of Ronkonkoma, and east of Huntington. More than 73% of the County&#x27;s land area and approximately 50% of its residents rely on diesel service that is less reliable, more polluting, and often requires riders to transfer at least once, and often multiple times, during their commute. I see a disturbing trend with Suffolk County routinely paying disproportionately more into New York State and MTA than we receive back in service and capital investment&#x27;whether through NYMTC capital funds, STOA operating funds or MTA capital funds. This imbalance must be addressed. Suffolk County residents and businesses pay a substantial share of MTA funding. In 2026 alone, Suffolk County taxpayers are contributing approximately $31.8 million for station maintenance across 41 stations&#x27;an average of $775,699 per station per year. Suffolk residents also pay some of the highest commuter fares in the nation. Monthly tickets now cost $394.50 from Ronkonkoma and Stony Brook, and $452.00 from Mastic, with daily fares reaching as high as $48.00. These increases come at a time when ticket options have become more restrictive, and an $8 fee is assessed for those who do not activate their tickets in a timely manner. Beyond fares, Suffolk County contributes significantly to the MTA through multiple dedicated funding streams, including the payroll mobility tax, sales tax, mortgage recording tax, motor vehicle registration fees, the petroleum business tax, and other sources. Congestion Pricing revenues reportedly exceeded expectations, generating approximately $550 million in the first year alone&#x27;and Suffolk County residents and businesses undoubtedly contributed significantly to that total. While I understand that ten percent of these funds are to be allocated to Long Island Rail Road capital improvements, this level of investment is only a starting point. Suffolk County needs more significant and transformative rail infrastructure funding. I applaud the MTA for several groundbreaking investments, such as the opening of Grand Central Madison, which has provided new service options and convenience for Long Islanders traveling to Manhattan, while also creating new opportunities for New York City residents to access Nassau and Suffolk Counties. Now, the MTA must turn its attention to Suffolk County. Suffolk County residents deserve greater rail access and more single-seat rides to both Penn Station and Grand Central Madison. I am calling for a comprehensive modernization of the LIRR in Suffolk County through strategic infrastructure investment in double-tracking and electrification. 37 2/3/2026 Edward Romaine Hauppauge NY Continued...available for just $10. Suffolk County, along with the Towns of Brookhaven, Smithtown, and Huntington, stands ready to work collaboratively with the MTA to begin the long-overdue process of improving this neglected branch. Additionally, the commuting needs of our East End residents must be addressed. Many do not travel to Penn Station or Grand Central Madison at all. The Montauk Branch requires upgrades, including increased track capacity, to allow the South Fork Commuter Connection to grow and serve even more East End travel demand and to better serve large-scale events such as the upcoming U.S. Open at Shinnecock Hills. The elimination of grade crossings in Suffolk County must be addressed. I appreciate the current collaboration between the MTA and Suffolk County on grade crossing elimination planning and prioritization. This work is essential for safety, reliability, and traffic congestion relief. Future federal funding opportunities must be pursued aggressively to advance these projects throughout Suffolk County. Thank you for taking these important points into consideration as you progress your capital program and federal funding agenda. Suffolk County residents deserve a modernized rail system and frequent, reliable train service options. I urge the MTA to commit to a bold, equitable vision for the future of rail service in Suffolk County. Respectfully, Edward Romaine Suffolk County Executive 38 2/3/2026 Edward Romaine Hauppauge NY 39 2/3/2026 Delilah Delgado The Bronx NY Suggesting new express bus line from West Side Highway. West Side commuters from Riverdale/Kingsbridge need a West Side Highway express bus to commute more effectively to work/recreational usage of the West side of the city. As of right now they only express buses to the East side of Manhattan. Please allocate MTA funds for this initiative. This will increase overall quality of life and when time is launch MTA will most certainly will see a return on investment! Good evening. On behalf of the Permanent Citizens Advisory Committee to the MTA (PCAC), thank you for holding this hearing on the MTA&#x27;s requests for federal grant funding in Federal Fiscal Year 2026. We strongly support the MTA&#x27;s proposals for funding capital projects across the LIRR, Metro-North, MTA Bus Company, Construction & Development, and New York City Transit. Now more than ever, it is critical that the federal government continues to fund and invest in transit&#x27;the backbone of New York and the entire region and country&#x27;s economy. The MTA&#x27;s capital projects are a key combination of system expansion and State of Good Repair upgrades. These projects cannot risk being delayed, or the millions of daily MTA riders will face the consequences of disrepair and lack of investment. The MTA network is over a century old, and it must be upgraded rapidly to ensure that future generations of New Yorkers can continue to rely on our subways, buses, and trains. In particular, riders need the federal government to support the MTA&#x27;s efforts to upgrade and expand its existing rolling stock. Purchasing more&#x27;and never&#x27;leaves buses is a major factor in ensuring that the MTA can run more service, with fewer delays and breakdowns. Signal upgrades, track and structure improvements, facility maintenance, and station repairs are other critical projects that the MTA needs federal fiscal support to complete. In addition, President Trump and the U.S. DOT must immediately disburse the Congressionally-appropriated funding for the Second Avenue Subway to ensure the program can continue. The MTA has done everything within its control to comply with DOT guidance, and continuing to delay and wholesale risking the project would be a disaster for thousands of working families who are employed by it and could soon be out of work, to say nothing about tens of thousands of residents of East Harlem who are one of the most transit-dependent neighborhoods in New York City and have been lacking a long-promised subway for nearly a century. It&#x27;s also critical for congestion pricing to continue. We&#x27;ve already seen what a massive success this policy has become, and the hundreds of millions of dollars that it has brought in for the MTA&#x27;s capital program are crucial to building a more accessible, expansive transit system. New York&#x27;and the entire country&#x27;cannot afford for this once-in-a-generation program to be canceled. 40 2/3/2026 Kara Gari Riders are counting on the leaders at all levels of government to invest in the MTA system. We enthusiastically support the MTA&#x27;s federal grant proposals for fiscal year 2026. Thank you for the opportunity to testify. In 2026, it is an unfortunate customers of the LIRR do not have free and reliable access to WiFi while onboard and on station platforms. The vast majority of ridership commute for work and would benefit from reliable internet for work and conference calls in our increasingly global economy. Additionally, with the new draconian ticket activation rules and less than 24 hour validation, many stations lack internet access making it difficult to purchase and/or activate. To support why I no longer purchase at home, today I was driving to Deer Park only to check the time and see it was cancelled. To ensure I could get to work, I drove back to Smithtown. These are two different zones with two different fares. This has happened in the reverse as well. Historically, I&#x27;ve saved my ticket to use another time. Are you going to reimburse me? All that to be said, I&#x27;m now only buying my ticket when I&#x27;m at the station and I know the train is coming. Please invest in WiFi infrastructure on the trains and station platforms. It is 2026 and the world is online. Time for the LIRR to catch up. 41 2/3/2026 Ashley Gzetic Smithtown NY Thank you for your consideration.

### [p291_c0001] Proposed Phase 2 stops for the Second Avenue Subway
[p291_c0002] Phase 2: 96th St (2nd Av)-125th St (2nd Av)/Broadway (125th St)*
[p291_c0003] • Subway stops for Phase 2: 106th St (2 tracks, 1 island platform), 116th St (2 tracks, 1 island platform), 125th St-2nd Av (2 tracks, 1 island platform)/Lexington Av-125th St (2 tracks, 1 island platform), Malcolm X Blvd (Lenox Av)-125th St (2 tracks, 1 island platform), Saint Nicholas Av-125th St (2 levels, 2 tracks, 2 side platforms), Broadway-125th St (4 tracks, 2 island platforms); Tunnel *Some of the stations listed in this Phase are part of an Expansion Project of Phase 2, it does NOT count as an additional Phase.
[p291_c0004] The 125th St-2nd Av stop is proposed to serve the 2nd Av/Queens Blvd (U) line. Between the 106th and 116th Sts stations will be a relay track beginning and ending with switches on each end.

## Page 295

### [p295_c0001] MTA 2026 Capital Projects Public Hearing:
[p295_c0002] February 3, 2026
[p295_c0003] Yesterday, I signed up to speak at today's hearing on the "MTA Capital Plan", but was never called, as I was watching for the full 55 minutes. I tried to type in the chat, but the person who asked if anyone was missing literally waited less than 5 seconds before she shut off the meeting. This was very disappointing and does not reflect well on your organization. Why wasn't I asked to speak, as I did receive confirmation which indicated that I was confirmed as a speaker? And why even bother to ask if anyone was missed, if you are going to close the meeting before I could spend 7 seconds to open chat and type? Anyway, here is my feedback.
[p295_c0004] Thank you for your continuing Park Avenue viaduct work, as I frequently take Metro-North on weekends and have never been delayed by your construction. This work is very important to the long-term future of all lines of Metr0-North.
[p295_c0005] I look forward to the construction of IBX in Brooklyn and Queens, as well as the extension of the 2 nd Ave subway to 125 th and Park and hopefully to the west side of Manhattan at 125 th and Broadway, several 5-year capital plans down the road. A new station at Broadway and 125 th could incorporate the 2 nd Ave subway, the "1" subway, Columbia, Riverside Park, and the west-side line could have a station at 125 th to serve Amtrak and/or Metro-North, in the distant future.
[p295_c0006] MTA should REPAIR the Beacon dock for the return of the ferry , and operate the ferry from at least mid-May to mid-November. I realize that the bus schedule between Beacon and Newburgh was recently expanded, but the ferry is needed for the redevelopment of the Newburgh waterfront. Orange County is not treated nicely by the MTA and the decision to let the pier deteriorate, then offer poor ferry schedules, certainly seems to promote the theory of the MTA letting the ferry service degrade and then hope no one would notice. Your failure to repair the Beacon dock is very damaging to the people and businesses of Orange County. You need to partner with politicians, business leaders, and passengers in Orange County and bring this important option back to Orange County by this summer.
[p295_c0007] MTA Capital should add a 2 car, possibly battery operated, shuttle from Hopewell Junction to Beacon on the former Maybrook line. This should be added to the next capital plan and could be started to serve rush hour commuters on weekdays.
[p295_c0008] The Hudson line, as various plans mentioned for 2 decades, needs a third track from just north of Breakneck Ridge to Poughkeepsie. This would be an 18-mile section, which already has a third track for 3 miles through Beacon, so that 3 rd track needs to be extended northward by 12 miles and southward by 3 miles. This would allow Amtrak trains to pass Metro-North trains would provide redundancy during trackwork and improve the flow of rail traffic.
[p295_c0009] Also on the Hudson line, the 3 rd rail electric needs to be extended from Croton-Harmon to Peekskill , on 2 of the 3 tracks, which is about 7 miles. This would allow electric trains to serve both Cortlandt and Peekskill, as Peekskill station already has 2 extra, dead-end tracks, where the trains would switch direction for their return to GCT. This would relieve pressure on the diesel trains, which are very full north of Croton-Harmon.
[p295_c0010] Likewise, third rail electric should be extended from Huntington to Port Jefferson on the LIRR, which is a distance of 23 miles.
[p295_c0011] Sincerely yours,
[p295_c0012] _Master Page # 295 of 496 - Finance Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 297

### [p297_c0001] DAILY NEWS NYDailyNews.com
### [p297_c0002] Friday, January 2, 2026 11
### [p297_c0003] Public Hearing
### [p297_c0004] CAPITAL PROJECTS PUBLIC HEARING
[p297_c0005] The Metropolitan Transportation Authority, on behalf of MTA New York City Transit and its subsidiaries; MTA Long Island Rail Road; MTA Metro-North Railroad; MTA Construction & Development; and MTA Bus Company (collectively referred to as the "MTA"), will hold a public hearing to solicit comments on MTAs federal grant proposals for Federal Fiscal Year 2026. This hearing will be conducted in a hybrid format, allowing participation both in-person and virtually via Zoom's online platform and conference call feature, and will include a livestream on the MTA 2026 Capital Projects hearing website.
[p297_c0006] The MTA receives federal funds pursuant to the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) of the U.S. Department of Transportation under Sections 5307, 5309, 5310, 5337, 5339 and 5340 of Title 49, United States Code (49 U.S.C. 5300 et seq.), as well as the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (Surface Transportation Act and other federal appropriations). The Governor of the State of New York, local officials, and publicly owned operators of mass transportation services have designated the MTA to be the recipient of these grants.
[p297_c0007] The requested funds are for categories of capital projects listed below and more fully described in the MTA Proposed Program of Projects. The MTA's Program of Projects is available for review at https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing or by calling the contact number provided below. The MTA solicits and encourages the comments of private transportation providers. Capital improvements under this Program generally take place within the MTA network or on State or city-owned property. Property acquisitions or relocations will be carried out in accordance with relevant laws and regulatory requirements. The projects included in this hearing are part of the approved 2020-2024 and 2025-2029 Capital Programs.
[p297_c0008] The capital projects have been, or expected to be, endorsed by the Metropolitan Planning Organizations for the New York metropolitan region and comply with the State Implementation Plan (SIP) as required by the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990. The projects are not anticipated to have any significant adverse environmental impact. If the FTA prepares a formal environmental impact statement on any of the proposed projects, the MTA will issue a public notice to make the statement available. All legal requirements related to senior citizens and individuals with disabilities will be met.
[p297_c0009] It is anticipated that any difference between the projects' cost and the federal grant amount will be covered through funds that are made available by any one or more of the following sources: State, local, affiliated agency funds (such as MTA Bridges and Tunnels or MTA); credits for non-federal project shares generated from toll revenues in accordance with federal statute (23 U.S.C. 120 (i)); or sales of property or program income.
[p297_c0010] In Federal Fiscal Year 2025, MTA received $925.4 million of Section 5307 funds, $1.221 billion of Section 5337 State of Good Repair funds, and $30.8 million of Section 5339 of Bus and Bus Facilities funds. Apportionments from Federal Fiscal Year 2026 are not yet available. Federal funds must generally be matched by a local share contribution for capital assistance of 20 percent for most funding categories.
### [p297_c0011] PROJECT CATEGORIES / ESTIMATED FEDERAL REQUEST (Dollars in Millions)

### [p297_c0013] Date, Time and Location of the Hybrid Public Hearing
[p297_c0014] Tuesday, February 3, 2026
[p297_c0015] Hearing begins at 6:00 P.M.
[p297_c0016] Speaker registration opens at 5:30 P.M. and closes at 6:30 P.M.
[p297_c0017] MTA Headquarters, 2 Broadway, 20th Floor, William J. Ronan Board Room, New York, NY 10004.
### [p297_c0018] Directions
[p297_c0019] By Subway: Take the 4 to Bowling Green, 5 to Whitehall Street, 1 to South Ferry or 2 to Broad Street.
[p297_c0020] By Bus: M15, M16 SBS, M20, M55, S1M1, S1M1C, S1M4C
[p297_c0021] By Staten Island Ferry: Take the Staten Island Ferry to South Ferry Terminal
### [p297_c0022] Public Hearing Format
[p297_c0023] This will be a hybrid public hearing with in-person participation as well as via Zoom's online platform and conference call feature, with a livestream available on the MTA 2026 Capital Projects hearing website https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing .
### [p297_c0024] Registering to Speak at the Public Hearing
[p297_c0025] Those interested in speaking at the public hearing must pre-register. Each registered speaker will have two (2) minutes to speak.
[p297_c0026] To register to speak you may 1) register online at https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing , or 2) call the Public Hearing Hotline at (646) 252-6777 (Telephone agents are available from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m. daily) or 3) sign up in person at the hearing. Written comments may be submitted and additional information requested through the same options listed above. Registration opens on January 2, 2026, and closes at 6:30 p.m. on February 3, 2026. All written comments must be submitted by 6:30 pm February 3rd. Comments will be transcribed and included as part of the permanent record of this hearing.
### [p297_c0027] To View a Copy of the Complete MTA Program of Projects
[p297_c0028] A complete list of projects eligible for funding under this proposal is available onsite and on the MTA Website at https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing . To receive a translated copy of the Complete MTA Program of Projects, please contact us by Tuesday, February 3, 2026.
[p297_c0029] Information on public viewing locations and on the projects eligible for funding can be found on the MTA website or by contacting the MTA at the address below. The MTA will also make the final program of projects available on its website at https://mta.info/transparency/grant-management for Section 5307 funded projects when the FTA awards the Federal Fiscal Year 2026 grants.
### [p297_c0030] Additional Ways to Comment or Request Information
[p297_c0031] By Postal Mail: To submit comments, for additional information, or to request copies of the application and final program of projects to be submitted to the FTA, letters should be addressed to: MTA Capital Program Funding / Grants Management, RE: 2026 Capital Projects Public Hearing, 2 Broadway, D16.94, New York, NY 10004.
### [p297_c0032] Accessibility and Language Assistance Services
[p297_c0033] At the public hearing, American Sign Language (ASL) and CART Captioning services will be available.
[p297_c0034] For Individuals Who Are Deaf or Hard of Hearing can use your preferred relay service or the free 711 relay service to connect to the Public Hearing Hotline at (646) 252-6777.
[p297_c0035] For Individuals Who Are Blind or Have Low Vision can request accommodations on or before January 26, 2026, submitting a request on line at https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing or by calling the Public Hearing Hotline at (646) 252-6777.
[p297_c0036] For Those Without Internet Access can dial into the hearing by calling the Zoom webinar at 1-646-828-7666 then enter Webinar ID: 160 726 7386, followed by the pound (#) sign.
[p297_c0037] For Language assistance or other accommodation: Please submit a request at least five business days before the hearing date online, by telephone, or by postal mail.
[p297_c0038] Metropolitan Transportation Authority
[p297_c0039] www.mta.info

## Page 299

### [p299_c0001] LUNES 5 ENERO 2026 El Diario NY | 7
### [p299_c0002] Audiencia pública
### [p299_c0003] AUDIENCIA PÚBLICA SOBRE PROYECTOS DE INVERSIÓN
[p299_c0004] La Metropolitan Transportation Authority, en nombre de MTA New York City Transit y sus filiales, MTA Long Island Rail Road, MTA Metro-North Railroad, MTA Construction & Development, y MTA Bus Company (denominadas colectivamente "MTA"), celebrará una audiencia pública para recabar comentarios sobre las propuestas de subvenciones federales de la MTA para el año fiscal federal 2026. Esta audiencia se llevará a cabo en un formato híbrido, lo que permitirá la participación tanto en persona como de forma virtual a través de la plataforma en línea y la función de conferencia telefónica de Zoom, e incluirá una transmisión en vivo en el sitio web de la audiencia sobre los proyectos de capital de la MTA para 2026.
[p299_c0005] La MTA recibe fondos federales de conformidad con la Administración Federal de Tránsito (Federal Transit Administration, FTA) del Departamento de Transporte de los Estados Unidos en virtud de los artículos 5307, 5309, 5310, 5337, 5339 y 5340 del Título 49 del Código de los Estados Unidos (49 U.S.C. 5300 et seq.), así como de la Ley de Infraestructuras Bipartidistas (Ley de Transporte Terrestre y otras asignaciones federales). 1 should be replaced with "La MTA recibe fondos federales de conformidad con la Federal Transit Administration (FTA) del U.S. Department of Transportation en virtud de las Secciones 5307, 5309, 5310, 5337, 5339 y 5340 del Title 49, United States Code (49 U.S.C. 5300 et seq.), así como de la Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (Surface Transportation Act y otras asignaciones federales).
[p299_c0006] Los fondos solicitados se destinan a las categorías de proyectos de capital que se enumeran a continuación y que se describen con más detalle en el MTA Proposed Program of Projects. El MTA's Program of Projects está disponible para su consulta en https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing o llamando al número de contacto que se indica a continuación. La MTA solicita y anima a los proveedores de transporte privados a que envíen sus comentarios. Las mejoras de capital en el marco de este programa suelen realizarse dentro de la red de la MTA o en propiedades estatales o municipales. Las adquisiciones o reubicaciones de propiedades se llevarán a cabo de conformidad con las leyes y los requisitos reglamentarios pertinentes. Los proyectos incluidos en esta audiencia forman parte de los Programas de inversión aprobados para los períodos 2020-2024 y 2025-2029.
[p299_c0007] Los proyectos de inversión han sido, o se espera que sean, respaldados por las Metropolitan Planning Organizations de la región metropolitana de New York y cumplen con el State Implementation Plan (SIP) según lo exige la Clean Air Act Amendments de 1990. No se prevé que los proyectos tengan un impacto ambiental adverso significativo. Si la FTA prepara una declaración formal de impacto ambiental sobre cualquiera de los proyectos propuestos, la MTA emitirá un aviso público para que dicha declaración esté disponible. Se cumplirán todos los requisitos legales relacionados con las personas mayores y las personas con discapacidad.
[p299_c0008] Se prevé que cualquier diferencia entre el costo de los proyectos y el monto de la subvención federal se cubrirá con fondos que se obtendrán de una o más de las siguientes fuentes: fondos estatales, locales y de agencias afiliadas (como MTA Bridges and Tunnels o MTA), créditos por participaciones en proyectos no federales generados a partir de los ingresos por peajes, de conformidad con la ley federal (23 U.S.C. 120 (i)); o ventas de propiedades o ingresos del programa.
[p299_c0009] En el año fiscal federal 2025, la MTA recibió $925.4 millones de fondos de la Sección 5307, $122.1 mil millones de dólares de fondos de la Sección 5337 para el buen estado de las infraestructuras y $30.8 millones de dólares de fondos de la Sección 5339 para autobuses e instalaciones de autobuses. Las asignaciones del año fiscal federal 2026 aún no están disponibles. Por lo general, los fondos federales deben complementarse con una contribución local del 20 % para la asistencia de capital en la mayoría de las categorías de financiación.
### [p299_c0010] CATEGORÍAS DE PROYECTOS / SOLICITUD FEDERAL ESTIMADA (en millones de dólares)
[p299_c0011]

```text
LONG ISLAND RAIL ROAD CONSTRUCTION & DEVELOPMENT Categorías de proyectos $M Categorías de proyectos $M Estaciones $244.00 Otras ampliaciones de la red $373.70 Vía 164.00 Total de MTA Construction & Development $373.70 Estructuras de línea 44.00 Comunicaciones y señalización 21.00 Talleres y patios ferroviarios 18.00 Energía 99.50 Varios 33.50 Total de LIRR $624.00 METRO-NORTH RAILROAD NEW YORK CITY TRANSIT Categorías de proyectos $M Categorías de proyectos $M Ferries $4.20 Vagones de Subway $6,850.00 Estaciones 60.76 Buques reemplazo 425.00 Vías y estructuras 256.30 Estaciones de pasajeros 3,280.00 Comunicación y señalización 301.12 Vía principal 80.00 Energía 10.00 Estructuras de línea 896.00 Talleres y patios ferroviarios 0.80 Señalización y comunicaciones 65.00 Varios 2.80 Talleres y patios ferroviarios 160.00 Grand Central Artery 134.34 Depósitos 220.00 Total de MNR $770.32 Vehículos de servicio 25.00 Varios 425.00 SIRTOA 71.00 Total de NYCT $12,586.00 MTA BUS COMPANY GRAN TOTAL DE MTA Categorías de proyectos $M $14,381.57 Bus de reemplazo 21.95 Instalaciones 5.60 Total de MTA Bus Company 27.55
```
### [p299_c0012] Fecha, hora y lugar de la audiencia pública híbrida
[p299_c0013] Martes, 3 de febrero de 2026
[p299_c0014] La audiencia comienza a las 6:00 p.m.
[p299_c0015] La inscripción de ponentes se abre a las 5:30 p.m. y se cierra a las 6:30 p.m.
[p299_c0016] MTA Headquarters, 2 Broadway, 20th Floor, William J. Ronan Board Room, New York, NY 10004.
### [p299_c0017] Direcciones
[p299_c0018] Por Subway: tome el 4 hasta Bowling Green; 6W hasta Whitehall Street; 1 hasta South Ferry o 4 hasta Broad Street.
[p299_c0019] Por Bus: M15, M16 SBS, M20, M56, SIM1, SIM1C, SIM4C
[p299_c0020] Por Staten Island Ferry: tome el Staten Island Ferry hasta la South Ferry Terminal
### [p299_c0021] Formato de la audiencia pública
[p299_c0022] Esta será una audiencia pública híbrida con participación presencial y a través de la plataforma en línea Zoom y la función de conferencia telefónica, con transmisión en vivo disponible en el sitio web de la audiencia de Proyectos de capital 2026 de la MTA https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing .
### [p299_c0023] Inscripción para intervenir en la audiencia pública
[p299_c0024] Las personas interesadas en intervenir en la audiencia pública deben inscribirse previamente.
[p299_c0025] Cada orador registrado tendrá dos (2) minutos para hablar.
[p299_c0026] Para registrarse para hablar, puede 1) registrarse en línea en https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing , o 2) llamar a la línea directa de la audiencia pública al (646) 252-6777 (los operadores telefónicos están disponibles de 6:00 a.m. a 10:00 p.m. todos los días) o 3) inscribirse en persona en la audiencia. Se pueden enviar comentarios por escrito y solicitar información adicional a través de las mismas opciones indicadas anteriormente. La inscripción se abre el 2 de enero de 2026 y se cierra a las 6:30 p.m. del 3 de febrero de 2026. Todos los comentarios por escrito deben enviarse antes de las 6:30 p.m. del 3 de febrero. Los comentarios se transcribirán y se incluirán como parte del registro permanente de esta audiencia.
### [p299_c0027] Para ver una copia del MTA Program of Projects completo
[p299_c0028] La lista completa de proyectos que pueden financiarse en virtud de esta propuesta está disponible en el lugar y en el sitio web de la MTA, en https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing . Para recibir una copia traducida del MTA Program of Projects completo, póngase en contacto con nosotros antes del martes 3 de febrero de 2026.
[p299_c0029] La información sobre los lugares de consulta pública y los proyectos que pueden financiarse se puede encontrar en el sitio web de la MTA o póngase en contacto con la MTA en la dirección que figura a continuación. La MTA también publicará el programa definitivo de proyectos en su sitio web, https://mta.info/transparency/grant-management https://mta.info/transparency/grant-management para los proyectos financiados por la Sección 5307 cuando la FTA conceda las subvenciones del año fiscal federal 2026.
### [p299_c0030] Otras formas de enviar comentarios o solicitar información
[p299_c0031] Por correo postal: Para enviar comentarios, solicitar información adicional o copias de la solicitud y el programa definitivo de proyectos que se presentará a la FTA, las cartas deben dirigirse a: MTA Capital Program Funding / Grants Management, RE: 2026 Capital Projects Public Hearing, 2 Broadway, D16.94, New York, NY 10004.
### [p299_c0032] Servicios de accesibilidad y asistencia lingüística
[p299_c0033] En las audiencias públicas, se ofrecerán servicios de lenguaje de señas estadounidense (American Sign Language, ASL) y subtitulado de traducción en tiempo real de acceso a la comunicación (Communication Access Realtime Translation, CART).
[p299_c0034] Las personas sordas o que tengan problemas de audición pueden usar su servicio de retransmisión preferido o el servicio gratuito de retransmisión 711 para conectarse a la línea directa de la audiencia pública al (646) 252-6777.
[p299_c0035] Las personas ciegas o con baja visión pueden solicitar adaptaciones hasta el 26 de enero de 2026, a través de una solicitud en línea a https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing o llamando a la línea directa de audiencias públicas al (646) 252-6777.
[p299_c0036] Las personas que no dispongan de acceso a Internet pueden conectarse a la audiencia a través del seminario web de Zoom llamando al 1-646-828-7666 e introduciendo el Id. del seminario web: 160 726 7386, seguido del signo numeral (#).
### [p299_c0037] Para asistencia lingüística u otras adaptaciones:
[p299_c0038] Envíe su solicitud al menos cinco días hábiles antes de la fecha de la audiencia por internet, por teléfono o por correo postal.
[p299_c0039] www.mta.info

### [p301_c0001] A10 廣告
### [p301_c0002] 世界日報
### [p301_c0003] worldjournal.com 2026年1月6日 星期四 THURSDAY, JANUARY 6, 2026
### [p301_c0004] 公開聽證會
[p301_c0005] Metropolitan Transportation Authority 將代表 MTA New York City Transit 及其分支機構 - MTA Long Island Rail Road, MTA Metro-North Railroad, MTA Construction & Development 及 MTA Bus Company (合稱“MTA”) 舉行公開聽證會, 以收集關於 2026 聯邦財政年度 MTA 聯邦撥款提案的意見。本次聽證會將以混合形式進行, 允許現場參與和通過 Zoom 的在線平台和電話會議功能以虛擬方式參加, 屆時活動將在 MTA 2026 年基本建設項目聽證會網站上進行直播。
[p301_c0006] MTA 根據 U.S. Department of Transportation 的 Federal Transit Administration (FTA) 依據 United States Code, Title 49 的 49 章 5307、5309、5310、5337、5339 和 5340 章 (“49 U.S.C. 5300 以下”) 以及 Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (Surface Transportation Act 和獲得聯邦基金。紐約州州長、當地官員和國有公共運輸服務運營商指定 MTA 為此類撥款的資金接收方。
[p301_c0007] 申請的基金用於下述類別的基本建設項目, 詳細描述請參見 MTA Proposed Program of Projects。要查看 MTA’s Program of Projects, 請訪問: https://mta.info/2026-capital-projects-hearing , 或致電下列聯繫電話。MTA 誠摯邀請并鼓勵私人交通運輸服務提供商提供意見和建議。該計劃的基本建設改良項目通常在 MTA 網絡或者州或城市所有物體內進行。物業收購或搬遷將根據相關法律法規要求執行。本次聽證會涉及項目均納入已獲批的 2020-2024 年和 2025-2029 年的基本建設計劃。
[p301_c0008] 基本建設項目已獲得(或預計將獲得)紐約 metropolitan 區 Metropolitan Planning Organizations 的簽字批准, 并且符合 1990 年《清潔空氣法案修正案》(Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990) 所規定的 State Implementation Plan (SIP) 要求。預計項目不會對環境產生重大不利影響。如果 FTA 準備針對任何提案項目發出正式的環境影響評價, MTA 將發布公告以發布環境影響。所有涉及高齡市民和殘障人士的法規要求都將得到滿足。
[p301_c0009] 項目成本與聯邦撥款之間的差額計劃將通過以下一種或多種資金渠道補足: 州、地方、附屬機構(如 MTA Bridges and Tunnels 或 MTA) 資金來源; 依據聯邦法規(23 U.S.C. 120 (i)) 取得的通行費收入的非聯邦項目股份貸款; 或者物業出售或項目收入。
[p301_c0010] 2025 聯邦財政年度, MTA 已收到 9,254 億美元的 5307 章基金, 12.21 億美元的 5337 章妥善維修(Good Repair)基金和 3080 萬美元的 5339 章 Bus and Bus 設施基金。2026 聯邦財政年度的資金分配方案尚未公布。對於大多數資助類別, 聯邦基金通常必須獲得相當於資助金額 20% 的當地資本援助份額。
### [p301_c0011] 項目類別及聯邦資金申請預估金額(單位: 百萬美元)
